title: Project 27DCT: The HUSL-Matrix Grand Unification and Temporal Reciprocity
abstract:
PROJECT NAME: Project 27DCT: The HUSL-Matrix Grand Unification and Temporal Reciprocity

PROJECT DESCRIPTION: ABSTRACT AND SCOPE
This repository serves as the primary technical anchor and immutable data record for the Holmes Universal Scaling Law (HUSL). It formalizes the discovery of the 19.412 Hz Universal Gate (Zm) and the corresponding 0.0515 s Temporal Pulse (T0). This project provides the fundamental derivations and empirical validation datasets for manifold coordination across quantum, geomechanical, and cosmological scales.

KEY DELIVERABLES
1, The Grand Unification Report: A comprehensive synthesis of Metric Coordination Theory (MCT) and HUSL axioms.
2, Temporal Reciprocity Proof: Mathematical derivation of the 1/T = 1/f identity and its role in resolving the Hubble Tension.
3, Predictive Alarm Logic (PAL): Framework for monitoring geomechanical phase-slip transitions (e.g., Reykjanes/Svartsengi corridor).

DATA SOURCES
Empirical validation is grounded in the following datasets:
- NANOGrav 15-Year Data Set (v2026.01): Validating spectral peaks and gravitational wave background scaling.
- Icelandic Meteorological Office (IMO): Real-time harmonic tremor feeds and seismic coordination nodes.

LEGAL AND INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY DISCLOSURE
EU AI Act Compliance (Art. 50) and Munich District Court Ruling (Feb 13, 2026):
All materials within this repository are the original intellectual creation of the human author, Lee Holmes (ORCID: 0009-0002-8135-0645). Generative AI is utilized strictly as a neuro-interface for linguistic formatting and structural accessibility to mitigate the effects of dyslexia. This methodology ensures scientific rigor while preserving the core intellectual property and original mathematical proofs of the human author.

LICENSE AND RIGHTS
Standard License: CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 International.
Statutory Protection: All rights reserved under German Copyright Law (UrhG Section 2).

title: Group bounded indirect reciprocity and social norms
abstract: preregistration

title: Assessing the Role of Appearance, Behavior, and Reciprocity for Meta-Liking Accuracy in Initial Social Encounters
abstract: In the present research, we will investigate whether people are able to accurately infer how much others like them. In addition, we will explore the processes underlying the formation of accurate meta-liking judgments, specifically self-observation of behavior, using feedback from others' behavior, and using the reciprocity heursitic. Using round-robin data, we will conduct Social Relations Model and Lens Model analyses.

title: Digital technology use and emotional problems in preadolescents: reciprocity and the moderating role of emotion regulation
abstract:
Digital screen and social-media use among children and adolescent has risen sharply in recent years (Ofcom, 2022), but our understanding of how and for whom digital technology use relates to mental health remains limited. Recently, using data from the DEvelopment of Emotional Resilience (DEER) study of preadolescents, we found that children spent an average 2 hours using screens on a typical weekday and that higher self-reported social media use was cross-sectionally associated with greater symptoms of anxiety and depression, poorer sleep, more somatic complaints, and lower life satisfaction, particularly in girls (Amir et al., in preparation). Those cross-sectional findings suggest that digital use is linked to multiple domains of child health, and prompt a follow-up question: what are the directions of those links over time?

Another observation from existing empirical findings in this area is that often, associations between screen time use and psychopathology can be weak and/or variable. This suggests that there are moderators that could amplify or attenuate associations. One of the recently developed frameworks for understanding the pathways behind digital use and psychopathology proposes that risky digital activity elevates depressed mood by provoking frequent, persistent negative affective (e.g., anger) and cognitive (e.g., “I feel stupid”) reactions (Sonuga-Barke et al., 2022). These associations are likely bidirectional, as depressed mood may increase engagement in risky digital activity, and digital stress can in turn increase negative affect and depressive symptoms. Importantly, the model also suggests that negative impacts may be mitigated when children either manage their digital behaviour or regulate the emotional and cognitive reactions it evokes. Based on this conceptual model and our earlier findings, we plan to explore the moderating effects of emotion regulation strategies on the association between digital use and internalising symptoms in preadolescents across time.

The key aims of our study are as follows: 1) to test whether screen time and internalising symptoms show reciprocal association across two timepoints (approx. 1 year apart); and 2) to test whether maladaptive emotion regulation strategies moderate those associations, i.e., where maladaptive emotion regulation alters how strongly screen use predicts later change in internalising symptoms and vice versa.

title: Investigation of Facial Images Shaped by the Reciprocity of Liking using the Classification Image Method
abstract: 相手から好意を向けられると，自分もその相手に好意を高め，相手の心理的特性および外見的特徴をポジティブに評価する（好意の返報性：Wilson & Henzlik, 1986）。しかし，相手が自分に対してどのような印象を持っているかという推定が相手の顔表象をどのように特徴づけるかは明らかでない。そこで本研究では「参加者自身に対して肯定的あるいは否定的な印象を持つ人物」の顔イメージをclassification image（CI: Dotsch & Todorov, 2012）を用いて画像化する（それぞれ，肯定顔CI，否定顔CI）。

title: Honor, Dignity, and Reciprocity: An Empirical Investigation of the Relationship between Cultural Logics and Positive Reciprocity
abstract:
This research explores the relationship between the cultural logics of honor and dignity and positive reciprocal behavior in adolescents. A quantitative study tests the hypotheses that the endorsement of honor and dignity norms would be differentially associated with direct, escalative, and indirect forms of positive reciprocity. We predict, that both are linked to direct reciprocity, and that the endorsement of honor is a stronger unique predictor of escalative and indirect reciprocity than the endorsement of dignity. 
A confirmatory factor analysis is calculated in advance to verify the factor structure of the forms of reciprocity.

title: Beyond “Deviance”: How Control and Reciprocity Shape Deservingness Through Dehumanization
abstract: The research examines deservingness and dehumanization in the context of drug overdoses in prison. The 2x2 experiment manipulates whether the prisoner is positively or negatively constructed based on the control and reciprocity portions of the CARIN scale, followed by questions on the deservingness and humanness of the prisoner. This approach bridges theories on social construction and dehumanization to understand which CARIN deservingness principles contribute to deservingness and operate through dehumanization in the context of a so-called “deviant” target population.

title: Study 3: Is indirect reciprocity intuitive? Testing the Social Heuristics Hypothesis on the domain of indirect reciprocity.
abstract:
This Study 3 will be based on the first two experiments of this same project. The methods are similar to Study 2 (two Prisoner’s dilemma games per participant), with the exception that this study doesn’t include the Cognitive Reflection Test.

Overall, the study project attempts to test the Social Heuristics Hypothesis of human cooperative decision-making in the context of indirect reciprocity. We are testing whether or not the reputation of the decision maker’s interaction partner has a larger effect on cooperation when the cooperation decision is intuitive rather than deliberative. 

In this online experiment participants play two separate one-shot Prisoner’s dilemma games with continuous strategies. Before making the decisions, participants will get to know their interaction partner’s decision in an earlier modified Dictator game with an anonymous third-party player. Participants’ use of intuitive vs. deliberative cognitive processes will be altered with Time pressure / Forced-delay manipulation: In time pressure conditions the participant will have a 10-second time limit on the decision screen, while in Forced-delay conditions there will be a 10-second forced-delay time before submitting the decision.

title: Age-related differences in responses to unfair offers in the ultimatum game: Links to attitudes toward reciprocity and risk for financial exploitation?
abstract: Little is known about how changes in how people respond to unfair outcomes differ across the lifespan. As people age, those who are more willing to accept unfair offers may be at greater risk for financial exploitation. To address this issue, we will have adults ranging in age from 21-80+ engage in an ultimatum game as a recipient with different partners while undergoing fMRI. Participants experience social conditions where they are partnered with a stranger and a nonsocial condition, where they respond to decisions made by the computer. Our behavioral analyses will examine the associations between decisions in the ultimatum game and the risk for financial exploitation. Our neural analyses will examine how brain responses to fair and unfair offers are associated with aging and risk for financial exploitation. Characterizing age-related differences in responses to unfair offers throughout the course of adulthood would be a significant step forward for understanding how younger, middle-aged, and older adults incorporate social information in their financial decisions.

title: Healthcare Shooting Perception: Framing, Reciprocity, and Prosocial Motive Attribution in Responses to Violence against Public Figures
abstract:
In December 2024, we conducted a rapid-response, between-subjects experiment in which we assessed U.S. American’s perception of the shooting of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson.
This study investigates how scenario framing (health insurance executive vs. business executive), individual differences (reciprocity norms, closeness to workers/executives, trust in the healthcare system, self-rated health), and rationalization/blame processes shape attributions of prosocial motives and justifications for violence against public figures. The broader aim is to understand how system-level grievances and personal/worldview variables interact to predict public perceptions of violent actions.

title: Cost- versus Benefit-based Reciprocity: Gender Differences and the Housework Mechanism
abstract: We study how people reciprocate kindness when the cost borne by the giver (sacrifice) and the benefit received by the recipient (payoff) can be varied independently. In a two-stage online experiment, participants first play a one-shot dictator game (endowment 100) sending an amount g to an anonymous partner without any mention of reciprocity (to capture “pure” kindness). Then, participants act as receivers in a strategy-method task, deciding how much to return for each possible (m,g) combination when the initial gift is multiplied by m and the receiver’s payoff becomes 100+mg. Our primary hypothesis is that women are more sensitive to the giver’s sacrifice (cost-based reciprocity), whereas men are more sensitive to the realised benefit (benefit-based reciprocity). We also test a theoretically motivated mechanism: household production. We randomise the timing/salience of a pre-task housework survey and, additionally, exploit regional variation in historical family-planning fine rates as an instrument for childbearing/housework intensity in exploratory analyses.

title: The Limits of Tolerance: A Matter of Simple Reciprocity? (Experimental Study 2)
abstract: In this between-subjects experimental study, we aim to replicate and extend previous work (https://osf.io/u2nrx) on how individuals define the boundaries of tolerance in the context of discussions on secularism, focusing on non-religious individuals in favor of secularism. Building on research highlighting the role of mutual, equality-based respect in fostering tolerance toward outgroups (Forst, 2012; Reininger et al., 2020; Simon & Schaefer, 2016; 2018), we manipulate two dimensions of outgroup respect attitudes: (1) whether a conservative-traditional Catholic outgroup expresses respect versus disrespect toward the non-religious ingroup, and (2) whether the outgroup’s attitudes toward a third group (Muslims) are unspecified, disrespectful, or respectful. Rather than implementing a fully crossed design, we focus on four experimental conditions that capture the theoretically most relevant combinations. Participants are asked to imagine a scenario in which the outgroup (1) disrespects their ingroup with no information about the third group provided (Condition 1), (2) respects the ingroup with no information about the third group provided (Condition 2), (3) respects the ingroup but disrespects the third group (Condition 3), or (4) respects the ingroup and respects the third group (Condition 4). Following previous studies, we expect that participants will express greater intolerance toward the outgroup when it disrespects the ingroup (Condition 1) compared to when it respects the ingroup (Condition 2). Likewise, we expect greater intolerance when the outgroup disrespects the third group (Condition 3) compared to when it respects the third group (Condition 4), holding respect toward the ingroup constant. Crucially, we further hypothesize an interaction: participants’ tolerance toward the outgroup should be constrained in the condition where it respects the ingroup but simultaneously disrespects the third group (Condition 3), compared to when it respects the ingroup and no information about the third group is provided (Condition 2). Finally, we explore whether these hypothesized effects are mediated by concerns about respect directed toward the ingroup and the third group, and moderated by perceived similarity and the inclusion of the third group within a superordinate identity.

title: Infants’ inferences of reciprocity from helping
abstract: In this study we ask if infants (14- to16-month-olds) expect a helping recipient to help the same helper back they have interacted with previously, rather than an individual who previously helped someone else.

title: The Effect of Trust Signal Clarity on Reciprocity: the Mediating Effect of Felt Trust
abstract: This study employs an experimental approach to examine the effect of trust signal clarity on reciprocity, and further to examine the mediating role of felt trust and the boundary conditions of this effect.

title: Investigating Cooperation in the Use of Protective Masks During the COVID-19 Pandemic:  It’s a Matter of Fairness and Reciprocity
abstract: The present study examines the social and emotional factors influencing people's decision to wear a protective facial mask, in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic, and the factors influencing people's evaluations of others' usage (e.g., what people will see poor mask usage as being most untrustworthy). The latter portion is a key point of novelty for this study. A major conceptual goal of the manuscript is to shed light on human cooperation, in general, which will carry implications beyond the ongoing pandemic.

title: Investigating stakeholder reciprocity in the age of AI
abstract: In this project, we research the effect of AI adoptation in stakeholder-and profit-oriented companies on the reciprocal behavior of stakeholders. We formulated three hypotheses and will collect data to test these hypotheses. Our study also includes an exploratory part to investigate the role of distrust and technosolutionism.

title: Ostracism, group identity, and reciprocity expectations
abstract: The current study aims to explore the effect of social ostracism followed by cooperative video gameplay on individuals in terms of self-esteem, belongingness, and reciprocity expectation. The study is done to test two distinct existing explanations for in-group favoritism: Social Identity Theory (SIT) and Bounded Generalized Reciprocity (BGR).

title: Who Needs Friends Like These: Group Reciprocity and Asymmetric Reactions to Betrayal
abstract: How do people respond when their countries' friends, partners, or allies let them down? Countries clearly have “friends”, and conversely, “enemies”; the US and Great Britain, China and North Korea, democracies and communist states and countless other groupings can serve as examples of what appear to be important “ingroups” and “outgroups” in international relations. Further, research across a variety of fields has also shown that people have high expectations for friends: we expect them to reciprocate positive acts and avoid negative acts that hurt us. This project explores whether individual-level effect of reciprocity in international contexts is moderated by group affiliation, specifically when a person's country is "betrayed" by a country considered a "friend", either by failing to return a favor or acting negatively. To date, we still do not understand how people respond to friendly countries violating reciprocity despite how common and important these instances are in the real world. I argue that people perceive a heightened sense of betrayal when an ingroup or "friend" country violates reciprocity against us, whether by failing to return a favor or executing a negative act against us. I hypothesize that if a country violates reciprocity by failing to return a favor, or initiates a negative act against a person's country first, people will more severely oppose future cooperation with and more strongly support negative acts against that country if the offending country is perceived as a "friend". This project uses a novel survey experiment on a representative sample of American citizens to evaluate the potential effect of group reciprocity on public attitudes in foreign affairs.

title: Guilt, Altruism and Reciprocity in Resource Allocation: An Experimental Study
abstract:
Introduction:
In this study, we explore the motives behind unexpectedly generous behavior, delving into the interplay of guilt, altruism and reciprocity in dictator game. We aim to disentangle these behavioral mechanisms and provide empirical evidence on the interplay of external randomness, strategic considerations, and potential feelings of guilt in distribution decisions.

Experiment Design:
We design an experiment with one control and three treatments, manipulating the initial endowment allocation between the allocator and the recipient to investigate the factors influencing resource transfers: no initial endowment, exogenous random allocation, allocator allocation and recipient allocation. 

For all treatments, the game consists of two stages, with the second stage being the dictator game, which is the same as the control setting. 
(1)Treatment X: For the first stage, the endowment allocation is exogenously and randomly determined between two options: Option 1, which is to the favor of the allocator, and Option 2, which is to the favor of the recipient. For the second stage, allocator allocates resources.
(2)Treatment A: For the first stage, it is the allocator who chooses between Options 1 and 2. For the second stage, allocator allocates resources.
(3)Treatment R: For the first stage, it is the recipient who chooses between Options 1 and 2. For the second stage, allocator allocates resources.

Hypothesis:
H1a When initial allocations are perceived as disadvantageous to the recipient, the transfers in the second stage will be higher in the Treatments (X, A, and R) than in the Control group, driven by the fairness preference. 
H1b When initial allocations are perceived as disadvantageous to the allocator, the transfers in the second stage will be lower in the Treatments (X, A, and R) than in the Control group. 
H2a Allocator’s self-serving initial choice will result in higher subsequent transfers in Treatment A than in Treatment X with the similar initial endowment, under the guilt-driven altruistic motive . 
H2b Allocator’s choice favoring recipient will lead to a reduction in the allocation in the second stage, compared with Treatment X with the similar initial endowment.
H3a Recipient’s choice favoring allocator prompts reciprocal increases in transfer in Treatment R compared to the random allocations in Treatment X, under the dynamic reciprocal motive . 
H3b Recipient’s self-serving initial choice will in lower subsequent transfers in Treatment R compared with the random allocations in Treatment X, under the dynamic reciprocal motive . 
H4a Compared with the scenario where the allocator makes an initial choice that benefits themselves in Treatment A, when the recipient chooses an initial allocation that benefits the allocator in Treatment R, the second-stage allocation in Treatment R will be higher.
H4b Compared with the scenario where the allocator makes an initial choice that benefits recipient in Treatment A, when the recipient chooses an initial allocation that benefits themselves in Treatment R, the second-stage allocation in Treatment R will be lower.

title: Same Behavior, Different Thinking: Direct Reciprocity Differences between Children with Autism and Children with Typical Development
abstract: Direct reciprocity is closely related to social behavior in children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and is an important guarantee of their integration into society. There are many differences in direct reciprocity between children with ASD and typical development (TD). Research has paid attention to the differences in reciprocity outcomes between the two types of children but has neglected the importance of the reciprocity process, which is a vital mechanism for eliciting reciprocal outcomes. Therefore, this study explored differences in direct reciprocity between children with ASD (n = 29) and TD (n = 34) from the perspective of outcomes and processes. The reciprocal outcomes of the two types of children were similar, both showing a pattern of ‘if you treat me well (badly), I will treat you well (badly),’ but their reciprocal processes differed. Children with ASD paid more attention to their own perspectives and responded based on their own gains and feelings. Conversely, children with TD paid more attention to others’ perspective and responded based on others’ motivations and feelings. This study holds substantial significance in addressing the reciprocity challenges faced by individuals with ASD and promoting their social inclusion.

title: Reciprocity and Friendship Jealousy
abstract: Friendships are valuable relationships. Having good friends has been linked to improved health, happiness, and longevity. But securing these lofty benefits requires us to find, make, and keep our valued friends. Recent work has posited that a friendship jealousy system is well-designed to help people meet a specific sub-challenge of keeping friends: preventing the defection of our valued friends to other people (third-party interlopers). According to this work, a primary input into the friendship jealousy system is Replacement Threat—indexed by cues that a friend is replacing oneself with an interloper (e.g., that the friend values another more than you; Krems et al., 2021, 2022). Yet in other work, friendships are built upon reciprocity—returning favors after they are given to you (Trivers, 1971; but see Silk, 2003). On this view, reciprocation failures hinder friendships and evoke negative friendship emotions. To begin to test these between ideas---toward answering questions about what evokes friendship jealousy and, more broadly, what friendships are for--we conduct a between-subjects experiment using U.S. online community participants from CloudResearch (CloudResearch-approved, 18+). Participants are randomly assigned to read one of three vignettes in which their best friends (a) reciprocate a favor (take the participant to the airport in bad traffic), (b) reciprocate a favor for a new friend but not for oneself, or (c) fail to reciprocate the favor. We expect that reciprocation will evoke happiness (but neither friendship jealousy nor anger), taking the new friend but not the participant will cue Replacement Threat and evoke friendship jealousy (as well as some anger, but not happiness), and simple non-reciprocation (refusing to reciprocate) will evoke anger (but neither friendship jealousy nor happiness).

title: Reciprocity of social mindfulness and well-being in adolescents: a cross-lagged design study
abstract: This study aims to investigate the reciprocity between social mindfulness and well-being in adolescents through a cross-lagged design. Adolescents from several schools in China will be recruited to participate, with data collection occurring at 3 time points, approximately four months apart. The primary focus of this research is to explore the mutual influence between social mindfulness and well-being over time. Additionally, the study will examine the mediating roles of key relationships, including teacher-student, parent-child, and peer relationships, as well as emotional empathy, in shaping the dynamic between social mindfulness and well-being. Expected outcomes include a deeper understanding of how social mindfulness and well-being influence each other and the contextual factors that may moderate or mediate this relationship.

title: Towards the limits of tolerance in polarized societies: A matter of simple reciprocity? (Experimental Study)
abstract: In this experimental study, we examine how individuals define their boundaries of tolerance, particularly in the context of differing opinions on migration policy. Our focus is on liberal individuals who support progressive migration policies and their (in-)tolerance toward those with conservative views on migration. Building on research highlighting the role of perceived equality-based respect in fostering respect and tolerance toward outgroups (Forst, 2012; Reininger et al., 2020; Simon & Schaefer, 2016; 2018), we investigate two key outgroup perceptions: (1) the perceived respect of conservatives toward the liberal in-group (respect reciprocity) and (2) their perceived respect toward migrants (respect generality). These factors will be experimentally manipulated in an artificial scenario in which participants adopt a specific perspective. We expect those perceptions of both respect reciprocity and respect generality to lead to higher levels tolerance and lower levels of intolerance. Moreover, we hypothesize that tolerance toward an outgroup will be constrained even when they are perceived as respectful toward one’s own group if they do not extend the same respect to a third group (i.e., migrants). Finaly, we explore whether these effects are driven by concerns for reciprocity and generality.

title: Towards the limits of tolerance in polarized societies: A matter of simple reciprocity? (Experiment - Pilot)
abstract: This is the pilot study for a planned experimental study that aims to study the limits of tolerance in the context of differing opinions on migration policy. Our focus is on liberal individuals who support progressive migration policies and their (in-)tolerance toward those with conservative views on migration. Building on research highlighting the role of perceived equality-based respect in fostering respect and tolerance toward outgroups (Forst, 2012; Reininger et al., 2020; Simon & Schaefer, 2016; 2018), we investigate two key outgroup perceptions: (1) the perceived respect of conservatives toward the liberal in-group (reciprocity) and (2) their perceived respect toward migrants (generality). These factors will be experimentally manipulated in four conditions of an artificial scenario in which participants adopt a specific perspective. In the pilot study, we aim to (1) test whether the manipulated scenarios match participants' perceptions of reciprocity and generality and (2) validate different measures of tolerance that we plan to use to measure our DV in the main study.

title: Reciprocity ans Self-relevance in Social Media Questionnaires (RSRSM)
abstract:
Soziale Medien (SM) nehmen immer mehr Einzug in unser Leben. So werden auch immer mehr Auswirkungen dieser vermehrten Nutzung sichtbar. Unterschieden wurde bislang größtenteils zwischen aktiver Nutzung, welche eher positiv mit dem Wohlbefinden korreliert und passiver Nutzung, welche vermehrt mit negativen Auswirkungen auf das Wohlbefinden assoziiert wird.
Verduyn et al. (2021) stellten die Hypothese auf, dass es bei dieser Unterscheidung nicht bleiben kann, da es innerhalb der aktiven und passiven Nutzung heterogene Ergebnisse gibt. 

In dem auf dieser Annahme beruhenden "Extended Active-Passive Model" der SM-Nutzung werden folgende Dimensionen angenommen: 
1. Reziprozität aktiver Nutzung und dessen Valenz (Wärme/Kälte der Interaktionen)
2. Selbstrelevanz bei passiver Nutzung von SM-Inhalten mit Bezug auf den konsumierten Erfolg oder Misserfolg anderer Nutzer.

Im Rahmen dieser Bachelorarbeit werde ich Items in vier Subskalen konstruieren, welche die Dimensionen nach Verduyn et al. (2021) erfassen sollen.
Ziel der Erhebung und Auswertung ist es, die Itemqualität anhand einer exploratorischen Faktorenanalyse zu ermitteln und die Skala auf ausgewählte Maße der Reliabilität und Validität zu überprüfen.
Je nach Skalenqualität werden weitere exploratorische Analysen bzgl. erster inhaltlicher Effekte durchgeführt.

Verduyn, P., Gugushvili, N., & Kross, E. (2021). Do social networking sites influence Well-Being? The extended Active-Passive model. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 31(1), 62–68. https://doi.org/10.1177/09637214211053637

title: A Matter of Reciprocity (RECIPROC II - Conjoint)
abstract:
In this experiment, we leverage a specific set of social policies: work-related policies. Work-related policies aim at influencing the situation of people who are in employment. Most notably, this includes policies targeting wage-levels and working-conditions for diverse occupation groups. While there is a growing literature on public preferences for social policy (Beramendi et al. 2015; Häusermann et al. 20211,2) we so far lack systematic evidence on public support for policies relating e.g. to wages, working conditions, and working hours. This is in spite of increasing evidence that these policies may have a major electoral impact (Marin 2021) generally, and that they drive a ‘wedge’ into the social coalitions supporting populist radical right-wing parties (PRRPs) specifically: strategically building on the support of both business owners and workers, PRRPs can be ‘generous’ on some issues (such as retirement pensions, Rathgeb 2024), but they need to systematically depoliticize issues pertaining to work relations in order to hold their support base together (Palier/Wagner 2023).
We hence formulate the explorative research question: what is the impact of work-related policy positions on support for programmatic platforms (a) among the public at large, and (b) among sub-groups? 
We employ a conjoint experiment to study this question, asking respondents to choose among programmatic platforms, each featuring randomized positions on a range of policy issues. These include issue positions on a total of four work-relates policies:
1. Increase minimum wage
2. Improve working conditions for essential workers
3. Reduce weekly working hours
4. Increase job security
Subgroup effects: For subgroup effects, we are firstly interested in socio-economic strata (education, income, employment status) and secondly, in political identity groups (party alignments, self-positioning on a left-right scale).
Other outcomes of interest: While our main interest relies in the effects of work-related policies, we include a multitude of other policy issues to account for a realistic choice set in contemporary party competition. Among these, we include several issues that have received little attention in the literature on  public policy preferences or on which there are ongoing contentious debates in the literature. As a secondary aim of the study, we intend to explore the effects of these issues on public support for programmatic platforms. 
These issues notably include: 
9. Decrease corporate tax
10. Increase wealth tax
11. Increase tariffs on imports

title: Social calibration of tie reciprocity status predicts centrality
abstract: The ability to accurately perceive the reciprocity status of social relationships predicts centrality in a social network

title: A Matter of Reciprocity (RECIPROC I)
abstract: Does increasing recognition for a group decrease their support for blaming outgroups? We study the scapegoating of welfare recipients, a strategy employed across Europe by the populist radical right to mobilize dissatisfied workers. Our study offers an experimental test of whether positive (negative) recognition offered by symbolic "deservingness" appeals affect perceptions of in-group and outgroup (un)deservingness alongside other political outcomes. The study will be conducted in the UK, France and Germany with N = 2,000 in each country. The experimental design will be a survey-based, between-subject randomized controlled trial with complete random assignment to 5 groups. Subjects will be randomly exposed to either of six treatments in the format of a hypothetical post on X/Twitter from a member of the parliament: (1) positive recognition of workers’ contributions; (2) positive recognition of workers’ needs; (3) negative recognition of workers’ contributions; (4) negative recognition of workers’ needs; (5) placebo (a short description of the daily work routine of a member of the parliament); or (6) control (a thematically unrelated post by a member of parliament). First, subjects will have to give their voluntary consent to participate in the survey. Then, subjects will fill out a short pretreatment survey measuring their demographic, social, and political attributes. Then, they will be randomly assigned to control or treatment. After exposure to treatment, the following outcomes will be measured. First, we will measure post-related outcomes (agreement and sharing intentions). Second, we will measure affective attitudes towards the ingroup and outgroups (workers, the unemployed, immigrants, managers, and politicians), using feeling thermometers. Second, we will measure public support for policies in favor of workers (increase minimum wages, improve working conditions, decrease weekly working hours), the unemployed, refugees, and companies. Third, we will measure perceptions of deservingness for the main outgroups of interest (unemployed and refugees). Fourth, we will measure self-evaluations of social status and populist attitudes. Finally, we will measure emotions experienced by the respondent.

title: Who Is Willing to Pay? Second-Order Beliefs and Reciprocity in Global Climate Cooperation
abstract: Climate change is one of the greatest threats to humanity. Meanwhile, it is also the global public goods problem par excellence (Stavins, 2011). Addressing this crisis requires collective action, which is contingent on individuals’ willingness to act and to financially support climate action (Andre et al., 2024). But what are the factors that drive people to contribute to this public good? This work examines the influence of pivotality, conditional cooperation and expected benefits on the individual willingness to act – specifically the willingness to pay 1% of the monthly household income to fight climate change. While laboratory studies suggest that perceived pivotality – a sense of making a critical difference – can enhance contributions to public goods, it remains unclear how the perception of pivotality works in the context of international climate action, where the global scale diminishes individual impact (Gee et al., 2024). Furthermore, the concept of conditional cooperation suggests that individuals may be more inclined to act if they believe others will do the same (Bechtel & Scheve, 2017). But while popularity and social norms might draw people in initially, it might be the belief that their personal contribution truly matters that drives individual action. To explore these dynamics, this research employs a vignette-based survey experiment conducted with a representative sample of adults in the United Kingdom, the USA and Germany. This work aims to contribute to the existing literature by further analyzing drivers for individual climate policy support.

title: Empra H WS24/25: The influence of neutral, negative and positive reciprocity on allocation decisions.
abstract: This is a replication study that we conducted  in the course of a seminar on the replication crisis.

title: Infants’ expectations about relationships and reciprocity
abstract: In this study we ask if infants (14- to16-month-olds) expect a helper to continue to help the same recipient they have interacted with previously, rather than an individual who previously received help from someone else.

title: Indirect Reciprocity Undermines Indirect Reciprocity Destabilizing Large-Scale Cooperation
abstract:
We ask how reputation-based indirect reciprocity sustain contributions to multiple public goods, each bound to a different scale of social interaction. Specifically, we are interested in testing whether cooperative equilibria at smaller scales of interaction can undermine cooperation at larger scales of interaction when group interests are not aligned across scales.

This is based on a pre-printed model which is available at: https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.11.07.566116.

title: Task-shifting, collaboration, and styles of reciprocity within professional healthcare networks and teams.
abstract: This study includes three paired conjoint experiments with forced-choice designs, aimed at examining collaboration motivation, work dynamics, and the influence of styles of reciprocity on work motivation within professional networks and groups.

title: Upstream Indirect Reciprocity and Inequality Aversion
abstract: This paper investigates the mechanisms of upstream indirect reciprocity through a laboratory experiment.  Specifically, we examine whether the inequality aversion model, which plays a fundamental role in studies of direct reciprocity, can also explain upstream indirect reciprocity.

title: Positive Reciprocity Given Ambiguity About Motives
abstract: Large-scale online experiment

title: Effects of helping efficacy and willingness to help on reputation and reciprocity
abstract: Willingness to help has been shown in previous research to affect one’s reputation as a cooperator and their likelihood of receiving help from others. Evolutionary theorists believe this happens because cooperative behavior in humans is only adaptive if they receive reciprocal help by selectively cooperating with other cooperators. However, reciprocal helping only provides a benefit if the attempt to help is successful. Therefore low helping efficacy may have similar consequences as low willingness to help. Even if an individual is willing to provide help, if they cannot provide useful help they may not earn the same boost in reputation or garner reciprocal help to the same extent as someone who can effectively help. This study examines whether the extent to which an individual can provide effective help (helping efficacy) influences their reputation and odds of receiving help in the future. We will also examine whether there is an interaction between the effect of helping efficacy and willingness to help on reputation and reciprocity.

title: Twitter Reciprocity Study
abstract: Study testing the effect of reciprocal open-mindedness on information selection online

title: Studying reciprocity based on welfare tradeoff ratios using the 2D λ slider
abstract: Preregistration of the experiment validating the 2D λ slider.

title: How Much for An Eye? International Security, Reciprocity, Cost, and Reputations
abstract: Why do countries seem to go out of their way, or pay a cost, to return a favor or sleight? Do people want to follow the logic of reciprocity, even in security situations, where the threat of “an eye for an eye leaves the whole world blind” looms large? My project investigates whether individuals supports the use of reciprocity in security contexts, even when it is perceived to be costly to themselves or their country to do so. More specifically, the primary hypotheses of this project center on whether whether two key variables, cost and visibility, affect individual willingness to support the use of reciprocity.  The study is grounded in theories of costly signaling, deterrence, and reciprocity. Costly signaling theory suggests that states engage in costly acts to reveal credible information about their intentions. Deterrence theory posits that states use reciprocal behaviors to deter opponents by demonstrating their willingness to engage in conflict. Reciprocity, viewed from a psychological perspective, suggests individuals follow behavioral rules of thumb, such as reciprocating actions regardless of costs, driven by emotions, moral obligations, and reputational concerns.

title: Indirect Reciprocity and Public Support for Cooperation
abstract: Why and when does the public support or oppose cooperation with other states, especially when their country is not directly involved? States constantly provide or deny cooperation with other states on issues that do not involve them, and these opportunities to cooperate span security, trade, and other areas of interest. The project build on previous research showing that public opinion has a major influence on these types of decisions, highlighting an important but underexplored factor that "moves" public opinion on cooperation: indirect reciprocity. This project explores whether humans are psychologically committed to following and enforcing the principle of reciprocity, otherwise known as the "golden rule" or giving an "eye for an eye", and do so even in international security contexts and even when their country is not directly involved. I hypothesize that if a country violates the "golden rule", or does not return a favor to another country, citizens from an observing country are willing to act as a third party and punish them for it by opposing security cooperation with them. This project uses a novel survey experiment on a sample of American citizens to evaluate the potential effect of indirect reciprocity on support for cooperation in the public.

title: The role of reciprocity in promoting honesty in children
abstract: This study aims to examine the role of mutual reciprocity between interviewer and child interviewee on children's truth-telling behaviour.

title: Influence of Priming the Norm of Reciprocity and Wording the Request for Help in a Human-Like Manner on Participants’ Intention to Help Robots
abstract: Influence of Priming the Norm of Reciprocity and Wording the Request for Help in a Human-Like Manner on Participants’ Intention to Help Robots

title: Reciprocity styles and work motivation
abstract: This study employs a paired conjoint design with forced choice to investigate whether and to what extent healthcare workers' work motivation is impacted by the reciprocity style of their supervisor, the majority of colleagues, and the majority of patients.

title: UCE Reciprocity
abstract: In this study, we seek to demonstrate the role reciprocity norms plays in the Underestimation of Compliance Effect.

title: Good Feelings For Bad Deeds - Suggested Reciprocity
abstract: This research seeks to understand how positive emotions, specifically feelings of gratitude, help direct the responses of beneficiaries to the dishonesty of others. Drawing on work showing that individuals react positively to the unethical behavior, particularly that which benefits them directly, this research seeks to understand why gratitude my arise from others dishonest actions. The project seeks to understand why beneficiaries may feel gratitude for the dishonesty of others, the boundaries of these reactions, and what sort of dishonest behaviors invite or suppress gratitude. Finally, this work seeks to understand how gratitude for the dishonesty of others is uniquely linked to positive reinforcement, helping to explain how these behaviors may be reinforced and encouraged by those who benefit from them.

title: How reciprocity in virtual interactions influences evaluation of interactive partners and behavioral and physiological responses
abstract:
The study investigates the role of recirprocal non-verbal behavior in social interactions. Participants interact with two virtual agents in a conversation-like paradigm in Virtual Reality.

Participants will interact with the virtual agents in a turn-like manner (one at a time). So called manipulation phases will be conducted where participants have to answer short questions asked by the virtual agents. In the manipulation phases, Zygomaticus activity as well as gaze behavior of the participants will be monitored. Depending on the reciprocity condition a detected smile or a detected direct gaze will result in reciprocal changes of the virtual agent (Reciprocal condition) or the behavior of the virtual agent will be independent of the participant's behavior (Random condition). 

In addition test phases (interleaved with manipulation phases and as an initial baseline) will be conducted where the agents answer on questions by the participants. During these test phases agents will show smiling and direct gaze and participants' responses to these events will be analyzed (mimicry and mutual gaze). Test phases are identical between recirpocal and random agents.
After baseline as well as after three initial and three final manipulation-test cycles ratings will be obtained for each agent.

Two female virtual agents will be presented (one reciprocal, one random; counterbalanced across participants. In addition, order of agents (reciprocal first, random first) will be counterbalanced across participants.

title: The cognitive foundations of prospective reciprocity
abstract: In the current study, we investigate the role of three cognitive capacities that might support prospective reciprocity: planning, patience, and risk tolerance.

title: Reciprocity and Self-relevance Social Media Activity Questionnaire (RSR-SMAQ)
abstract:
Soziale Medien (SM) nehmen immer mehr Einzug in unser Leben. So werden auch immer mehr Auswirkungen dieser vermehrten Nutzung sichtbar. Unterschieden wurde bislang größtenteils zwischen aktiver Nutzung, welche eher positiv mit dem Wohlbefinden korreliert und passiver Nutzung, welche vermehrt mit negativen Auswirkungen auf das Wohlbefinden assoziiert wird.
Verduyn et al. (2022) stellten die Hypothese auf, dass es bei dieser Unterscheidung nicht bleiben kann, da es innerhalb der aktiven und passiven Nutzung heterogene Ergebnisse gibt. 

In dem auf dieser Annahme beruhenden "Extended Active-Passive Model" der SM-Nutzung werden folgende Dimensionen angenommen: 
1. Reziprozität aktiver Nutzung und dessen Valenz (Wärme/Kälte der Interaktionen)
2. Selbstrelevanz bei passiver Nutzung von SM-Inhalten mit Bezug auf den konsumierten Erfolg oder Misserfolg anderer Nutzer.

Im Rahmen dieser Bachelorarbeit werde ich eine Subskala zum "Social Media Activity Questionnaire" (Ozimek et al., 2023) konstruieren, welche die Dimensionen nach Verduyn et al. (2022) erfassen soll.
Ziel der Erhebung und Auswertung ist es, die Itemqualität anhand einer exploratorischen Faktorenanalyse zu ermitteln und die Skala auf ausgewählte Maße der Reliabilität und Validität zu überprüfen.
Je nach Skalenqualität werden weitere exploratorische Analysen bzgl. erster inhaltlicher Effekte durchgeführt.


- Ozimek, P., Brailovskaia, J., & Bierhoff, H. (2023). Active and passive behavior in social media: Validating the Social Media Activity Questionnaire (SMAQ). Telematics and Informatics Reports, 10, 100048. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.teler.2023.100048
- Verduyn, P., Gugushvili, N., & Kross, E. (2022). Do social networking sites influence Well-Being? The extended Active-Passive model. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 31(1), 62–68. https://doi.org/10.1177/09637214211053637

title: Hot vs Cold reciprocity
abstract:
Unforeseen emotions triggered by unexpected events can push individuals away from the actions they planned. Overall, in a non-emotional Cold state, people tend to misjudge how they would behave in an emotional Hot state, which leads to what has been called hot-cold empathy gaps by psychologists. We explore the role of a hot-cold empathy gap for reciprocation in a trust game. 

Specifically, in a one-shot binary trust game (TG), we study whether a hot mode of interaction leads to different behavior than a cold mode of interaction. In the hot TG, the second mover first gets feedback about the choice of the matched first mover and then makes her choice to reciprocate or not. In the cold TG, the second mover is asked to state her plan of action. That is, she is asked whether she reciprocates or not in the case that the matched first mover trusts. 

According to standard economic theory, no difference should be observed in the second mover's reciprocation rate between a hot and a cold decision mode. However, in a hot decision mode, unforeseen emotions may arise when the second mover observes that the matched first mover trusts, thus influencing the second mover's choice. These emotions may be underestimated in a cold decision mode. Thus, the reciprocation rate of second movers may differ under the two decision modes. More precisely, the emotion that we want to investigate is Surprise. Intuitively, if the second mover unexpectedly observes that the first mover trusts and is positively surprised by this move, she may be more inclined to reciprocate than if her choice would be elicited in a cold way. If so, the more surprised she is by trust, the more likely she will reciprocate trust. When the second mover's choice is made under the cold decision mode, surprise cannot influence the choice because it is difficult for the second mover to foresee this emotion.

title: Hot versus cold reciprocity-2nd experiment
abstract: We performed the experiment pre-registered at https://osf.io/2856n as planned. Contrary to the 'surprise hypothesis', reciprocation rates were higher in the Cold than in the Hot treatments. The current research aims at investigating the mechanisms that can rationalize these findings.
